exemplar.title: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

detailedExemplarData.title: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

strengths: Sustained focus on the prescribed title throughout, with every paragraph explicitly linked to the ethical injunction
Effective use of discipline-specific concepts (e.g. replication crisis, provenance, colonial historiography) to connect to History and the Human Sciences
Clear, coherent arguments supported by specific and relevant examples (IQ/eugenics, mask-mandate research, Holocaust denial, oral histories)
Awareness and evaluation of different points of view, including utilitarian, consequentialist and deontological perspectives
Consideration of implications for knowledge reliability and societal harm, showing higher-order analysis
weaknesses: Occasional repetition of ideas (e.g. duplicate utilitarian perspective) affecting concision
Limited depth in critiquing opposing perspectives and in methodological analysis of examples
Stylistic imprecision and informal phrasing in places, detracting slightly from academic tone
Vague criteria for evaluating which contradictions are âmeaningful,â and incomplete sentence fragments
Insufficient discussion of concrete strategies (peer review, preregistration) to mitigate biases
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay consistently meets the descriptors for band 7â8: it maintains a sustained, focused discussion of the title, forges effective links to two areas of knowledge, and offers clear, coherent arguments supported by well-chosen examples. There is awareness and evaluation of differing viewpoints and careful attention to the implications of these arguments. Minor weaknessesâin repetition, depth of critique, and stylistic precisionâprevent the response from reaching the sophistication expected in band 9â10. Awarding 8 out of 10 accurately reflects the strong performance demonstrated in the strand assessments while acknowledging the areas for improvement outlined in the annotations.

annotations.comment: The utilitarian perspective is stated twice in nearly identical terms; consolidating these sentences would improve concision and avoid redundancy.
The criteria for evaluating which contradictions are âmeaningfulâ remains vague; the student should propose clear standards or methods for this evaluation.
The sentence fragment ending âhighlighting socioeconomic factors, asâ is incomplete and obscures the argument; revise to complete the thought for clarity.
While the IQ/eugenics case is relevant, the student could elaborate on how socioeconomic variables were empirically assessed to strengthen the exampleâs analytical depth.
The mask-usage example would benefit from critical evaluation of Lu et al.âs methodology and sampling biases to deepen the analysis of contradictory results.
The discussion of bias could be strengthened by outlining institutional checks like peer review or preregistration that combat subconscious prejudices.
Insight into power structures shaping historical evidence demonstrates strong awareness of how validity is socially constructed.
To conclude more powerfully, link the implications of ethical engagement to concrete practices in policy-making or pedagogy.
The informal phrase âover all unfairâ undermines academic tone; revise with precise language such as âdisproportionately unjustâ.
Good identification of the replication problem in psychology, highlighting how cognitive biases impede engagement with contradictory evidence.
The essay contrasts utilitarian ethics but should also differentiate deontological principles here to enrich the ethical analysis.
Consider discussing specific strategies (e.g., preregistration, blind analysis) to illustrate how confirmation bias can be mitigated in human-science research.
The conclusion synthesizes arguments well but could be enhanced by posing an explicit knowledge question to steer further reflection.
The introduction effectively situates the ethical directive within both history and the human sciences, establishing clear context and relevance for the prescribed title.
The student should sharpen the knowledge question by explicitly defining the scope of âethical obligationâ in each area of knowledge to guide analysis more precisely.
The criminology example identifies conflicting studies but lacks citation of specific research or data; referencing named studies would substantiate the claim.
The essay effectively articulates the epistemological tension between thorough evaluation of evidence and timely decision-making in public health contexts.
The reference to âpsychology classâ is too colloquial; replace with a formal citation of Lu et al. (year) to reinforce academic credibility.
The deontological perspective is invoked but not defined; a brief explanation of duty-based ethics would clarify its application to historiography.
The colonial historiography example powerfully illustrates how omission of evidence distorts narrative, effectively linking to the ethical imperative.
The example of Holocaust denial aptly highlights the need for historians to differentiate credible contradictions from misinformation.
The discussion of oral histories would benefit from analysis of verification challenges and the methods historians use to corroborate such sources.
Consider integrating reference to ethical guidelines (e.g., UNESCO principles) for oral history to deepen the treatment of this topic.
The aphorism âhistory is written by the winnersâ effectively captures the power-bias dynamic and its impact on narrative credibility.
The term âinterdisciplinary perspectivesâ is mentioned without examples; illustrating collaboration between historians and human scientists would strengthen this claim.

The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
